1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California

Attention: 
Regina Kutchins 
Gentlemen: 
Pursuant to a telephone discussion with Regina Kutchins 
and on behalf of a corporate clien}!', we hereby request an informal 
opinion of the Commission as to wl'iether our client (the "Corporation") 
has made a "contribution" or is af "committee" within the meaning 
of the Fair Political Practices Act. 
The facts, as represented by 
the Corporation, are as follo,-1s: 
1. 
The Corporation owns a commercial property including 
land and a building (the "property"). 
The property was leased 
under a 15-year lease commencing in 1969 at the rental rate of 
$2,500 per month. 
The original tenant vacated the property in 
July 1974 and litigation is now pending with respect to the lease. 
The property has been vacant since July 1974, except for a short-
term lease terminated by the tenant after six months' occupancy in 
}\ugust 1975. 
2. 
In March 1976 the Corporation was approached by the 
campaign manager of a political candidate (the "candidate"), who 
asked if the property was available for rent as a campaign head-
quarters for the candidate. 
The Corporation indicated that the 
property was available, provided that an agreement could be worked 
out whereby the Corporation would have the opportunity of terminat-
ing the candidate's tenancy in the event that a more attractive 
tenant Crone along. 
The campaign manager offered $200 per month as 
a rental. 
and 
3. 
The parties entered into a lease pursuant to which: 
(a) 
Occupancy commenced on April I, 1976; 
(b) 
The term of the lease was 7-1/2 nonths; 
(c) 
The total rental of $1,500 was 
d in advance; 
(d) 
The Corporation was given the right to terminate 
the lease for any reason on five days' notice and agreed in such

case to refund a pro rata share of the entire rental. 
4. 
At the time the lease was negotiated and made, the 
officer of the Corporation who negotiated the lease knew nothing 
about the political affiliation or views of the candidate or what 
o 
ce he was running for. 
The only other officer of the Corpora-
tion who participated in the decision to lease the property was 
similarly disinterested in the candidate, except as a prospective 
source of rental income. 
Since that time, neither of such officers 
nor the Corporation has done anything to support the candidate or 
his campaign, and has no intention of doing so. 
5. 
The Corporation's sole purpose in entering into the 
transaction was to derive some rental income from the property, 
\,"'hile at the same time maintaining the flexibility to rent the 
property on more advantageous terms. 
review of the Fair Political Practices Act leads me to 
conclude that the Corporation would have a duty to disclose under 
the Act only if it ""ere determined that its entering into the lease 
constituted a "contribution" by a "committee. 
The opinion of the 
Commission with regard to these and any other relevant questions, 
based on the foregoing facts 
is requested. 
My understanding from 
Regina Kutchins is that we can expect an oral opinion from the 
Commission within a few days after the Commission's receipt of this 
letter. 
Please feel free to call me at any time. 
Ri chard L. Seidenwurm

sections.question: whether our client (the "Corporation") has made a "contribution" or is a "committee" within the meaning of the Fair Political Practices Act
sections.question_synthetic: Does a corporation that leases commercial property to a political candidate's campaign at below-market rates constitute a "contribution" or "committee" under the Fair Political Practices Act?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from attorney Richard L. Seidenwurm on behalf of a corporate client, not an FPPC response. The routing slip indicates FPPC staff advised the client is not a committee based on these facts, but that is marginal notation, not the formal advice letter. Document is from May 5, 1976. No formal conclusion section present in the request letter itself.

embedding.summary: A law firm requests an FPPC informal opinion on behalf of a corporate client regarding whether leasing vacant commercial property to a political candidate's campaign headquarters at $200/month constitutes a reportable contribution or committee formation under the Fair Political Practices Act.
